perturbation.explanation: Changing the date to before the effective date of the agreement creates a legal inconsistency, potentially invalidating the agreement due to backdating concerns.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification violates basic contract law principles, particularly regarding the elements of contract formation, including offer, acceptance, and consideration. The agreement specifies an effective date of December 9, 1996, but the signature dates are now set earlier. Changing the signature date to before this effective date can lead to legal challenges, as it implies the agreement was ratified before it was officially in effect, creating ambiguity and potentially undermining the validity of the contract, implying a lack of mutual assent at the stated time.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification creates a legal inconsistency because it changes the contract date to before the agreement's effective date, potentially invalidating the agreement. The Law Explanation indicates this can lead to challenges regarding mutual assent and contract validity. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on 'consideration,' the Law Explanation's point about the signature date preceding the effective date impacting 'mutual assent' suggests a fundamental problem with the contract's formation, directly contradicting basic contract law principles.

perturbation.original_text: This amendment to Section 2 (titled "Term"), Part B of the Co-Branding Agreement is made effective December 9, 1996 by and between PC Quote, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "PCQ") and A.B. Watley, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "ABW"), who are also the parties contracted in the aforementioned Co-Branding Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This amendment to Section 2 (titled "Term"), Part B of the Co-Branding Agreement is made effective retroactively to January 1, 1995 by and between PC Quote, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "PCQ") and A.B. Watley, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "ABW"), who are also the parties contracted in the aforementioned Co-Branding Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Retroactively applying the contract to a date significantly before its execution can create legal issues related to obligations and liabilities during the period before the agreement was actually made.
perturbation.law_explanation: Retroactive application of the contract can be problematic because it may impose obligations or liabilities on parties for actions taken before they agreed to be bound by the contract. Contract law generally requires mutual consent and understanding for a contract to be enforceable. Applying the agreement retroactively could lead to disputes about whether the parties truly intended to be bound by the terms of the contract for the period before its execution. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts emphasizes the importance of mutual assent and clear understanding, which can be jeopardized by retroactive application.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text retroactively applies the contract to January 1, 1995, which contradicts contract law principles requiring mutual consent and understanding. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this retroactive application can create legal issues related to obligations before the agreement. The Law Explanation emphasizes the importance of mutual assent, jeopardized by retroactive application. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that a contract is an agreement creating mutual obligations enforceable by law, requiring mutual assent. Applying the contract retroactively imposes obligations for actions taken before the agreement, undermining mutual assent, leading to a clear contradiction of contract law principles.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall apply to said PCQ and ABW and all of their subsidiaries and related companies.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall apply to said PCQ and ABW and all of their subsidiaries and related companies, regardless of whether such subsidiaries or related companies existed at the time of the original Co-Branding Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the amendment to entities that did not exist at the time of the original agreement raises legal questions about consent and privity of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially violates the principle of privity of contract, which generally states that only parties to a contract (and certain intended beneficiaries) have rights and obligations under it. Extending the amendment to subsidiaries or related companies that didn't exist at the time of the original agreement could be viewed as an attempt to bind entities that did not provide consent or consideration, creating a lack of mutual assent. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts emphasizes the need for mutual agreement and bargained-for exchange, which may be absent for these newly included entities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-204. Formation in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-204. Formation in General. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement , including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract . (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the amendment's applicability to subsidiaries and related companies that did not exist when the original Co-Branding Agreement was made. The LLM-provided Law Explanation states that this potentially violates the principle of privity of contract because it attempts to bind entities that did not provide consent or consideration. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses agreement as being essential to the formation of contracts. Extending the contract to entities that did not exist at the time of the original agreement may mean those entities did not agree to the original terms, thus creating a contradiction. This falls under the definition of inconsistencies, where the modified text violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.
